consent:
Welcome!

We are Dr. Mario D. Molina, a faculty member in the Division of Social Science at New York University Abu Dhabi, and Dr. Mauricio Bucca, a faculty member in the Department of Sociology at Universidad Catolica de Chile. We are conducting a research study to find out more about the processes involved in people’s attitudes towards a variety of social and political topics and issues.



Your participation in this study is completely voluntary. If you agree to participate, you may be asked to read an article on a current issue/topic and you will be asked to answer a series of questions regarding your opinions, preferences, and beliefs, as well as provide us with some basic demographic information. The whole process should take about 7 minutes of your time.



Your participation in this study does not involve any risk to you beyond that of everyday life. You are not likely to have any direct benefit from being in this research study; however, taking part in this study may help researchers better understand people’s attitudes about various social and political issues and how those attitudes are formed.



Participation in this study will involve no cost to you. You will receive 1.35 pounds for your participation at the end of this session. While you can skip questions in the survey that make you feel uncomfortable and you can withdraw at any time by just exiting the survey, keep in mind that you must complete all of the questions in the survey in order to be paid. Otherwise, you will forfeit your payment.



This study is designed to be completely anonymous. We will not ask for your name or any other directly identifying information. All study data will be collected through an online survey platform called Qualtrics. Qualtrics is a secure platform that adheres to rigorous privacy and security standards, holding certifications such as SOC 2 Type II and the ISO 27001 series. Any data you provide will be encrypted during transmission using current security protocols like Transport Layer Security (TLS) (the technology behind HTTPS). Access to the collected data on Qualtrics is restricted to the study investigators. Furthermore, to enhance your privacy, this survey has been configured so that Qualtrics will anonymize responses, meaning IP addresses will not be collected or will be masked, making them unavailable to the investigators.



If you have any questions about this study, you may contact Dr. Molina (mdmolina@nyu.edu) or Dr. Bucca (mebucca@uc.cl) by email. For questions about your rights as a research participant, you may contact the IRB office and refer to the project HRPP-2023-109 at New York University Abu Dhabi, +971 2 628 4313 or IRBnyuad@nyu.edu.




If you wish to participate, please select the ACCEPT button below to begin the survey. If you do not wish to participate in this study, please select the DECLINE button, and your session will end. 

Sincerely, 
Dr. Mario D. Molina and Dr. Mauricio Bucca

fairineq_low_1:
Currently in the UK, higher-income groups earn about four times as much as lower-income groups, with the former making about £65 thousand a year while the latter make about £15 thousand a year.
 

In your opinion, what would be a fair distribution of income between higher- and lower-income groups? Constraint: The two boxes must always sum to £80 thousand. - Higher-income group (thousand £)

feedback: My parents are from working-class backgrounds but were able to benefit from policies in the 1950s and 60s allowing them to gain education and jobs not accessible to many younger generations. Even though I grew up in a comfortable household and went to an excellent. university, I have struggled to translate this into a good job and lifestyle. There are so many factors at play here: family background for sure, but also age, health, gender, disability, parental status and luck plays a significant factor too. Very interesting survey, thank you!

In the UK our political spectrum does not go from "liberal" to "conservative" - a liberal is a centrist and the political party in power, Labour, is a social democratic party to the left of our liberal party. If you want responses from a UK audience, suggest you tailor your survery questions to be more globally appropriate.

feedback: Wealthy and well educated (and/or ambitious) parents who pay for their children to be privately educated are automatically giving a disproportionate advantage as their children will be mixing with power and influence, and will be sheltered from poverty and inequality.  Of course it is possible to be upwardly mobile, and this is clearly evidenced by the large proportion of Asian children who go to university and then enter high paying professions, parental aspiration is a huge driver here.  Peer group pressure amongst the working class is still prevalent, with a fear that children who embrace education will then be moving into a world where the working class are left behind, so there is fear that allowing children to do well at school is a recipe to lose your child into a world not understood.  This is a very tricky problem, and in spite of the article at the beginning of this study, class and education are still major predictors of future wealth.  Recruiters recruit people like them, if they are white, male, privately educated then there is little chance for a black, working class, state educated woman to land the job.
